items.why: Students confuse storage with circulation. Blood carries glucose; muscles and liver store glycogen (the storage form). Liver glycogen regulates blood sugar; muscle glycogen fuels local contraction.

items.q: Is a tennis serve a closed or open skill?
items.correct: Closed - performed in a predictable, self-paced environment.
items.misconception: Open - because tennis is a fast-paced sport.
items.why: Students judge by the sport, not the moment. The serve happens at the server's pace with a stationary ball - closed. The return of serve is open. Skill classification depends on the SPECIFIC action's environment.

items.q: When you flex your elbow, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: Students confuse agonist (the prime mover doing the action) with antagonist (the opposing muscle that relaxes). In elbow flexion, biceps is the agonist; triceps is the antagonist (and vice versa for extension).

items.q: How many bones are in the adult human skeleton?
items.why: Correct answer: 206. “106” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Hip. “Elbow” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Number of bones in the adult human body?
items.why: The real figure is 206 bones. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
